title: Why Making Mistakes in Math Is a Good Thing
author: Tklesson Team
content:
A third grader completes a worksheet of multiplication problems. They hand it to a parent for checking. The parent points to an incorrect answer. The child's face falls. They grab the paper back, erase the answer vigorously, and mutter that they are bad at math. The parent rushes to reassure them. But the moment has already done its damage. The child has learned that mistakes are bad, that a wrong answer means something negative about their ability, and that math is about getting everything right the first time. This belief is not just wrong. It is harmful. Mistakes in math are not a sign of failure. They are one of the most powerful tools for learning. Here is why, and how to help your child embrace them.

What Happens in the Brain When You Make a Mistake

When a student makes an error in math, their brain does something remarkable. It registers a mismatch between what it expected and what actually happened. This mismatch triggers increased attention and heightened neural activity. The brain is primed for learning in the moments after a mistake. If the student is then shown the correct answer and understands why they were wrong, the learning that occurs is deeper than if they had gotten the answer right the first time.

Researchers have observed this process directly using brain imaging. When a student makes an error, there is a spike in electrical activity in the brain. This spike does not occur when the student answers correctly. The brain is essentially saying, pay attention, something unexpected just happened, we need to figure this out. The mistake creates a learning opportunity that a correct answer does not.

This has profound implications for how math should be taught and learned. A worksheet that comes back with every answer correct is not evidence of excellent learning. It may be evidence that the work was too easy. A worksheet with a few errors that the student then corrects and understands is evidence of learning happening at the right level of challenge. The errors are not the problem. They are the point.

Why Children Develop the Belief That Mistakes Are Bad

The belief that mistakes indicate low ability is learned, not innate. It is taught, often unintentionally, by the way adults respond to errors. A parent who sighs when they see a wrong answer is communicating disappointment. A teacher who marks an answer wrong without explaining why is communicating that the error is the end of the conversation. A classroom culture where students compare scores and celebrate perfect papers is communicating that only flawless performance is valued.

Children internalize these messages quickly. By second or third grade, many have already formed the belief that being good at math means getting everything right. When they inevitably encounter a problem they cannot solve immediately, they conclude that they are not good at math. The conclusion is premature and incorrect, but it shapes their behavior. They avoid challenging problems. They give up quickly when stuck. They stop trying, because trying and failing feels worse than not trying at all.

Breaking this cycle requires changing the messages children receive about mistakes. The change begins with the words adults use when a child gets something wrong.

How to Respond to Math Mistakes

Instead of marking an answer right or wrong and moving on, engage with the error. Ask the child to explain how they arrived at their answer. Often, the mistake is not random. There is a logical process behind it, and the error occurred at a specific step. When the child talks through their thinking, they may catch the error themselves. If they do not, the adult can see exactly where the thinking went off track and address that specific point. The response is not this is wrong. It is let us look at how you got here.

Normalize mistakes by sharing your own. When you make an error, whether in math or in daily life, point it out without shame. I added wrong just now. Let me figure out where I went off track. The child who sees an adult treating mistakes as normal and correctable learns to do the same.

Praise the process, not the outcome. Instead of you are so smart for getting that right, say you worked really hard on that problem, or I saw you try three different strategies before you figured it out. The praise shifts the child's focus from fixed ability to effort and persistence. A child who is praised for effort is more willing to attempt difficult problems than a child who is praised for being smart.

Use the phrase not yet. When a child says they cannot do something, add the word yet. You cannot do long division yet. The word transforms a permanent statement about ability into a temporary statement about the current stage of learning. The child is not incapable. They are in progress.

Celebrate the mistakes that lead to learning. When your child catches their own error and corrects it, acknowledge that moment. You found the mistake and fixed it. That is exactly what good mathematicians do. The celebration shifts the emotional experience of making a mistake from shame to competence.

The Bottom Line

Mistakes in math are not evidence of low ability. They are the moments when the brain is most ready to learn. Children who fear mistakes avoid challenge and limit their growth. Children who understand that mistakes are part of learning persist longer, tackle harder problems, and ultimately learn more. Respond to errors with curiosity, not judgment. Praise effort and persistence. Use the word yet. Celebrate the correction. The goal is not a child who never makes mistakes. It is a child who knows what to do with them.
